sourceMovie.title: Midori
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Midori (1992) stuck because it commits to its animation premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Hiroshi Harada's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The animation and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Belle
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Midori clicked for you, Belle (2021) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and drama, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Visitor Q
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Midori clicked for you, Visitor Q (2001) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and horror, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Midori?
faq.answer: Try Belle, Visitor Q, Kizumonogatari Part 1: Tekketsu—each shares DNA with Midori while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Midori?
faq.answer: Midori (1992) is best described as Animation, Drama, Horror. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Midori worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.1/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this animation lane.
